speaker: interviewer
text: The boom is on channel one, correct?

speaker: interviewer
text: That is correct. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Perry Goodfriend. P-E-R-R-Y G-O-O-D-F-R-I-E-N-D. Today's date is March 31st, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Jacob Szczupak. And we are in Atlanta, Georgia, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Camera is rolling. Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Szczupak, could you say your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Jacob Szczupak. J-A-C-O-B. And last name, S-Z-C-Z-U-P-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakub or Yankev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yankev in Yiddish, and Jakub in Polish. Jakub spelling is J-A-K-U-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family nickname is Kuba. K-U-B-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your birth date and how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am right now 60. I was born January the 1st, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How far back can you remember? What's your earliest memories of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The room we lived in Warsaw. The first memories I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: The boom mic's coming up on channel two.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Boom mic should be on channel one. Can we just-- excuse me a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to rewind?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Oh, here, that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Szczupak, what's your earliest memory of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, my earliest memories are the room we lived in. The room, I remember, was divided, divided sort of a half. In one half was a little, little iron stove where my mother used to cook something. And I remember the earliest remember-- memories are when my mother used to knee in front of the bed and I was standing in the bed before going to sleep. And I used to repeat after my mother the prayer before to go to sleep-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- and I was repeating this. This is my earliest memories is the apartment. I remember some singers used to-- beggar-singers used to come to the yard and sing. And my mother used to wrap up some money and ask me to throw it to the person who was singing. The room was rented from an old Jewish couple. They were living in a very small place, in a small room. They were renting the bigger room for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These beggars, were they Jewish beggars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they were all walks of-- I mean, Jewish, non-Jewish, it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of religious background did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents, if I can recall, were, were Orthodox religious. My parents were not Orthodox religious, were not. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went to synagogue in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I don't remember for them to go to synagogue, but I know they used to-- they attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names or places? You were just a small child. Do you remember any names or places or neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. I remember my father's family came from a little village in East Poland. And my father was unemployed. I think he was unemployed. So he sent, because he didn't earn any money, he send me and my mother to his, to his mother, to my grandmother. And we were staying there as long as he did not have a job. And I remember Thursday. It was before Shabbos Friday. My grandmother used to bake bread. And she baked for me a small bread, especially for me. I remember my grandmother from my mother's side. Very poor. Very poor. They lived in the one room. Along the walls were three beds in one small room. There was one window. The window was not going outside, but it was in front of this window was a wall from a next house. Very poor. It was always dark there, always dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town in East Poland that you went to, what was the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The father's family came from [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a village. It's not-- the next city is Wlodawa. It's-- the administrative center was Wlodawa. And it was in Lublin-- district of Lublin. My mother came also from the same district, from Lublin, but she came from, I think, it was a typical Jewish city named [NON-ENGLISH]. But I cannot recall this-- how the cities looked like, you know. I just-- I remember these rooms, these places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Szlomo, Shloime Szczupak. My mother's name was Miriam or Maria, Mary, [NON-ENGLISH] from home, but also Szczupak after marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do when he-- how long was your father unemployed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was a child. I don't, I don't remember how long he was employed. He was a waiter. He was working as a waiter in Warsaw. My mother used to tell me that he used to work sometimes 16 hours per, per day in a very famous restaurant named [NON-ENGLISH]. And when he used to come home in the morning, his, his shirt was wet from sweat. But when he was unemployed-- I think he, he was unemployed because it was a tough time. But also he was later involved in a union, a union of the waiters. And probably the owner of this restaurant just let him go because of his involvement, involvement in the, in the unions. That's how I can recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was, what was his involvement with the unions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was probably a member of the unions, or union, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other interests did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Throughout his life, he loved to read, especially in Yiddish. In my home, always was spoken only Yiddish. And in interests, he loved to tell jokes and listen to jokes, to have a good time. Many, many friends. He had many, many friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what kind of people-- do you remember people coming by your apartment? Any kind of people come by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Neighbors, we had neighbors. Non-Jewish neighbors. It was not a Jewish center. The neighbor's boy once, he was, he was much older than me. He was, I think, 14. He took me on a bike and drove around, you know. We had very good friends. We were good neighbors. I, I cannot recall, ever recall this, who used to come to our house, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewer
text: The heat just came on. We could turn it off real quick. Is it on? Is that OK? It has a definite, different sound to it. Let's keep [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Should we go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll go on. Tell me about your mother. What did she enjoy doing? What did she like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's enjoyment, my mother's enjoyment was also reading and singing. She, she, she was singing Yiddish songs. She was a, a treasure chest of Yiddish songs, Yiddish sayings, proverbs. That's what-- and of course also like to be in a, in a company. And as I mentioned before, reading, you know. That's all that I can recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so there's a lot of Yiddish in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, and it was spoken only Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a natural thing to speak only Yiddish at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents liked to read Yiddish and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sing Yiddish songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their leisure time, yes. And singing when it's-- when there's a company, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, as a company?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the company used to get together with family or friends. We used go to her-- they always asked my mother to sing something in Yiddish. And she, she enjoyed it to do it. She enjoyed it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Tell me about your other friends. You mentioned the 14-year-old with the bicycle. Do you remember any other friends from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very good place for my mother to go with me to, to, to the park and feed me there, because we spent a lot of time apart during the summer in Warsaw Park, Saski Park. And that's how I just-- very, very, very little memories. Very little from those before the war. I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this family that you lived with, were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I mean, they rented the room for us. Yes, they were Jewish. Older couple. They were a older couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember meeting any other family members? You've mentioned your grandparents a little bit. Any other family members that you met at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very little. I remember name, Serkie. My mother used to come with me to Serkie. It was her relative. It was in a different part of the city, I think. Serkie, she was a cousin. Serkie had some kids. It was a girl, I think 15, 16. She was dating. I-- it's, it's something that it's really, I cannot even-- it was a very joyful house, I remember. There was a lot of laughter in this house we used to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Not, not before the war, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can you tell me about life in Warsaw for you before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I remember, I remember when Hitler invaded Poland and they were bombing over Warsaw. In the middle of the street, they used to make bunkers, you know. I don't know how to say the right in English, you know. It's just holes that people could leave their homes and hide there, not to be bombed or killed by the German bombs. And, and there were a lot of people sitting there in this hole. And I remember it was a woman with a child. I don't remember the woman, I don't know. But I remember the child was crying. And my daddy risk his life. He run out from the bunker, ran to our apartment because he remembered we had some milk for the kid. And he brought the milk for this crying child. This, I don't remember, but my mother used-- told me that when it was a bombing and I was with my daddy, my daddy covered with his, with his body, me. So in case if, if it will be shooting, he will be killed, not me. My mother told me that it was-- we were on the streets, I don't know even how, and a wall fell down from the bombing. And I was covered with, with, with rubbish. My mother was covered till, till just the head of hers was out. And after the bombing, people rescued us, you know. I was not harmed. My mother was also not harmed. Some, some with the lucky. We were lucky, but it was, you know, bombing. But these are things-- it was a few days after the Germans entered Warsaw. Jews still not yet had to wear the yellow [NON-ENGLISH], the Mark of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, that's right. And Warsaw was already out of food. And my mother, since she did not look Jewish, she approached a German passenger car. I think it was an Opel. I can't recall. And she asked, she asked if she can get a piece of bread for the child. And they, they gave her a piece of bread with, with some, with some marmalade or something, and jelly. But this I don't remember exactly. My mother told me. [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- let's go back just a little bit. You mentioned that your mother used to sing Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Throughout her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- when you were a child, do you remember any songs that she used to sing to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- most of them, I don't remember. Some, I, I, I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there one song in particular that you remember her singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it's a love song, and I think from the city where she came from. There is a little house standing in a small, small street. And over there, on the attic apartment, lives [NON-ENGLISH], my girl. And every evening, I'm, I'm in front of her house. And I'm whistling for her to come down, but her mother says not to respond. It's not nice to whistle. It doesn't fit to a Jewish boy to whistle. And [NON-ENGLISH] sewed for me a, a little sack for the, for the prayer book and [NON-ENGLISH] with the [? Mark of ?] David on it, especially for him as a gift. And he listens to her, the small feet, how they're, they are running down the stairs to see him. But then, all of a sudden, oh, god, the boy says, it was just a dream. It was a sweet dream. There was some-- so many songs. [NON-ENGLISH], which is very popular songs. They are songs who I cannot sing and I cannot even repeat it. They were songs who were not recorded anywhere. There were even songs created after Hitler came to power, creating Yiddish songs that-- a song in Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the Jews lived a good life before Hitler came to power. And this was-- my mother used to tell me, this was, was, was singing a Jewish beggar, a blind man who came to the yard and was singing this. Apparently, he was a refugee from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she remember that and sing it for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember this, but my mother used to sing this. There were many songs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there one that you could sing a little bit for us now that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not in mood to sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's OK. So, you mentioned the bombing beginning in Poland and Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were bunkers in the streets that people used to go out into. They were actually in the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Of course, people had to hide, you know, had to hide somewhere, because who knows which bomb will fall on this particular house. That's why they run out from the houses, sometimes on the back yard, on the yard, sometimes in the middle of the street. It was made already, during the invasion of Poland, these hiding places. It was not-- a lot different hiding places. Warsaw was not prepared for it at all. It was all done, I think-- that's what I found out later-- later, after the invasion of Poland. You know, the, the people made, made some hiding places for-- against the, the air raids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any harassment or was there any kind of life change for you that you can recall when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it get rougher for you in some ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, yes. Apparently. I didn't feel it. I was a child. I mean, maybe-- probably I was hungry, but this kind of stuff you don't remember. I remember my daddy was drafted to the military, to the Polish military after the invasion. And he was in the military, apparently only for 10 days. And afterward, he was demobilized, came back. And I think from the drapes, which covered-- divided our room, my mother and father apparently made some sacks to put on their shoulders to leave Poland, to leave Warsaw. First, my daddy left to the East. He said, that's probably the best thing for Jews to do, is to leave Warsaw and go to the East, to Russia. On his way, he stepped into the place where he used to live, where he came from. By the way, this all that I found out later, obviously. And he stepped in to his mother and his sister, Sura, [? and her ?] mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mother was too old. She said she doesn't want to go anywhere. But Sura went with him. On the border, they slept somewhere. And Sura woke up next day. She said she doesn't want to go anymore. She had to go back. My father asked, why? I had a dream. The dream told me to go back. And of course, she disappeared during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went to, to Russia. And he worked over there in Brest, Brest-Litosvk-- it was named into Russian-- as a waiter. And then, apparently-- how he done it, I don't know-- he sent for me and for my mother, his wife. And we went, we went, obviously, illegally. We crossed the border between Poland and Russia. As we know now, Russia had an agreement with Germans, the Nonaggression Pact. And it was illegal to cross the border from Poland to Russia. But some, some soldiers there, apparently-- that's what my mother said-- looked away. They pretended they don't see the refugees crossing the border. Yes. And, of course, we came to Brest-Litovsk. And we lived there, I think, a few months or a half-year, a year, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we, before we leave Brest, I want to go back to before you were leaving Poland. Do you remember any kind of discussion? I mean, you mentioned the sacks being made from the drapes. But do you remember any kind of discussion about why, or even later, that your mother might have told you about why, when the decision was made, why the decision was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the conversations. Apparently, there were conversation between mother and father. But I don't remember these conversations. I was too, too, too tiny. Too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me also about-- you mentioned that you went with your mother to catch up with your father. Tell me a little bit about the journey for you and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the journey. Obviously, the border, the border between Poland and Russia was moved far to the west, because Russians obviously took away a huge part of Poland in 1939. The journey, I don't remember. But I do remember that we were crossing the border, night time, and the border, the earth, the soil of the border was plowed, you know, so that those who were standing on the border, guarding the border, would see steps if somebody crosses the border illegally. And that's what I remember. My mother used to-- told me to be quiet-- shush, still-- that I shouldn't say a word. And they were crossing the border. The border, yes. And that's what I remember-- night, these, these walking through the freshly--plowed fields, and my mother saying, [NON-ENGLISH], don't say a word, you know, be quiet. I remember that we were not alone. There were another group of also refugees crossing the border. We were not just, just alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there somebody leading the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Apparently, yes. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other children being in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you carry with you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I even don't remember this. I know I had a tag here, with apparently my name and address and whatever. This I remember, attached in case if I will get lost or whatever, to find me.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]
